pages.title: Welcome to Ransomware & Backup Awareness
pages.subtitle: Learn how ransomware can affect daily operations, why backups matter, and how employees can reduce business risk.

video.caption: Introduction to ransomware and backup awareness (replace later)

pages.title: What is ransomware?
pages.text:
Ransomware is a type of cyberattack where criminals lock, encrypt, or make files and systems unusable until a payment demand is met. For a small business, that can mean losing access to customer records, invoices, scheduling systems, shared drives, email, or even the computers employees rely on every day.

Ransomware is not just an IT problem. It can disrupt sales, operations, payroll, customer service, and trust.

pages.title: How ransomware often gets in
pages.text:
Ransomware frequently begins with everyday mistakes, weak security practices, or exposed systems.

  • Clicking a phishing email link or attachment
  • Using weak or reused passwords
  • Logging into compromised websites or fake portals
  • Using outdated systems or software
  • Opening suspicious files from unknown senders
  • Leaving internet-facing systems unpatched

Attackers often look for the easiest way in, which is why employee awareness and basic cyber hygiene matter so much.

Real-world examples:

SamSam: CrowdStrike says SamSam was observed using unpatched server-side software to enter environments. This is a good reminder that ransomware does not always begin with one bad click. In some cases, attackers get in through a vulnerable internet-facing system, create a backdoor, move through the network, and then launch the ransomware later. SamSam was also linked to the 2018 attack on the city of Atlanta, where major city services were disrupted.

DoppelPaymer: CrowdStrike describes DoppelPaymer as a later ransomware family related to BitPaymer. CrowdStrike’s reporting also connects it to Dridex-linked intrusion activity, showing how attackers may first gain access, learn the environment, move laterally, and only then deploy ransomware across a larger organization.

Ransomware does not always start with one bad click. It can also begin with an unpatched system or an earlier malware infection that gave attackers time to prepare.

Reference: CrowdStrike: Ransomware Examples

pages.title: Close to home: an Ohio example
pages.text:
Ransomware incidents do not only happen to large organizations. In Ohio, Kettering Health publicly dealt with a ransomware attack that disrupted operations and led to the cancellation of some elective inpatient and outpatient procedures.

Why this matters: One cyberattack can interrupt normal work, delay services, create confusion, and put heavy pressure on the organization responding to it.

Even if your organization is smaller and in a different industry, the lesson is the same: ransomware can cause serious disruption close to home, and preparation matters before an incident happens.

Reference: WLWT coverage of the Kettering Health ransomware incident

Project Playbook is not associated with WLWT.

video.caption: WLWT coverage of the Kettering Health ransomware incident

pages.title: Did you know?
pages.text:
• Ransomware appeared in 44% of breaches analyzed in Verizon’s 2025 Data Breach Investigations Report, up from 32% the year before.
    Source: Verizon article on the 2025 DBIR
  • Verizon also reported ransomware attacks rose 37% year over year.
    Source: Verizon article on the 2025 DBIR
  • Chainalysis reported a 35% year-over-year drop in ransomware payments in 2024, and said fewer than half of recorded incidents resulted in a victim payment.
    Source: Chainalysis 2025 ransomware payments article
  • IBM reported that the average cost of an extortion or ransomware incident remained high at $5.08 million when the incident was disclosed by an attacker.
    Source: IBM Newsroom article
  • A real-world example: Reuters reported UnitedHealth said the Change Healthcare ransomware attack affected about 190 million people, and Reuters separately reported the company expected the attack to cost up to $1.6 billion.
    Source: Reuters on people affected
    Source: Reuters on estimated cost

Ransomware is still widespread, and even one incident can create major operational and financial damage.

pages.title: Why backups matter
pages.text:
Backups help an organization recover important files and systems after ransomware, accidental deletion, or other disruptions.

But backups only help if they are properly managed.

Good backup habits include:

  • Back up data regularly so less work or information is lost.
  • Test backups to confirm they actually work before an emergency happens.
  • Protect backup access so attackers cannot easily change or delete backup copies.
  • Keep backups separated so ransomware cannot encrypt everything at once.

A backup strategy is part of cybersecurity because it helps reduce damage when prevention fails.

pages.title: What NIST says about ransomware readiness
pages.text:
NIST does not just recommend having backups. It recommends planning, implementing, testing, securing, and isolating backups so they are more likely to work during a ransomware incident.

  • Test backups regularly: A backup is only useful if it can actually be restored when needed.
  • Secure and isolate important backups: If backups are always exposed to the same environment, ransomware may reach them too.
  • Keep response and recovery plans ready: Organizations should know who to contact and what steps to follow during an incident.
  • Prepare before an attack happens: Recovery is easier when roles, contacts, and backup processes are already in place.

According to NIST, backups should not only exist — they should be tested, protected, and separated from everyday risk.

NIST References:

  • NIST: Protecting Data from Ransomware and Other Data Loss Events
  • NIST: Preparing Your Organization for Ransomware Attacks
  • NIST: Tips and Tactics – Ransomware
  • NIST: Getting Started with Cybersecurity Risk Management – Ransomware

image.alt: NIST ransomware readiness and backup guidance illustration (replace later)

pages.title: What employees should do
pages.text:
If you suspect ransomware or unusual system activity, act quickly and follow your organization’s reporting process.

  1. Stop interacting with suspicious files, links, pop-ups, or messages.
  2. Disconnect only if your organization instructs you to do so or if your incident process requires it.
  3. Report the issue immediately to IT, security, management, or your incident contact.
  4. Do not try to hide the problem or fix it on your own unless that is part of your role.
  5. Share what you noticed, including error messages, file changes, suspicious emails, or when the issue started.

Fast reporting can reduce damage and help protect the rest of the organization.

CISA also recommends preparing before an incident happens. That includes creating backups of critical systems and data, using multi-factor authentication, patching systems and software, developing incident response and business continuity plans, conducting cybersecurity risk analysis, segmenting critical systems, using application allowlisting, and performing penetration testing.

Helpful reporting contacts for organizations:

  • Report to CISA: CISA: I’ve Been Hit by Ransomware!
  • Cyber incident response guidance: cisa.gov/cyber-incident-response
  • Ransomware guide: CISA Ransomware Guide
  • Find your local FBI field office: fbi.gov/contact-us/field-offices
  • Find your local Secret Service field office: secretservice.gov/contact/field-offices

Reference: CISA: I’ve Been Hit by Ransomware!

image.alt: CISA reporting and ransomware contact guidance

pages.title: Report it quickly and know who to contact
pages.text:
The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) advises organizations to report ransomware incidents quickly and not assume that paying will solve the problem.

Why not pay?

  • Paying does not guarantee recovery. You may not get your files or systems back.
  • Paying can encourage more attacks. It shows that ransomware can be profitable.
  • You may still face more demands later. A payment does not always end the incident.

For Ohio local governments: Ohio law places restrictions on ransomware payments. A political subdivision experiencing a ransomware incident generally cannot pay unless its legislative authority formally approves that decision and states why it is in the entity’s best interest.

Do not treat paying the ransom as the default response.

Helpful reporting contacts for Ohio-based organizations:
